site-title: Keupstraße: A study of fragmented memory
timeline-m1:
The Kuaför from the Keupstraße
A documentary film by Andreas Maus
timeline-m3:
Open letter to Otto Schily
and the Federal Committee of Inquiry
Cologne, 13.3.2013
timeline-m4: This statement was broadcast on the evening of June 10, 2004 in the 8 p.m. edition of the "Tagesschau".
timeline-m5: From the fall of the Berlin Wall to the nail bomb
timeline-m6: A publication of the Amadeu Antonio Foundation
context-1:
On June 9, 2004, the NSU carried out a nail bomb attack in Cologne's Keupstraße, a center of Turkish business life, in which 22 people were injured.
This project asks how events can be forensically reconstructed when evidence has been systematically destroyed or suppressed.
context-2: It is based on the assumption that memory itself is a form of evidence: in individual experiences, emotional traces and collective meaning formation. By analyzing memories in terms of memory system, coding modality and processing state, the project investigates how truth becomes visible beyond institutional archives and what responsibility this entails for society.
mosaic-popover: Download information on categories and classification in the report under "Research".
body-research: This research collects direct testimonies about the 2004 nail bomb attack in Cologne. It is not the event itself that is being reconstructed, but the narrative field that surrounds it, as a multi-perspective reconstruction. The project aims to give a voice to the fragmented memories of Keupstraße. Discursive mapping is used to analyze how the perception of the attack has been anchored in the collective consciousness for more than two decades. From the perspective of cognitive and clinical psychology, I would like to examine what kind of memory is reflected in the discourse.
desc-files: Investigation files of the NSU trial (investigation documents on the NSU trial).
desc-press: Media coverage: Ten years of prejudice (Der Spiegel).
desc-interviews: Direct discussions with those affected, investigators and experts.
desc-video: Documentary: "The Kuaför from the Keupstraße" (Film Archive).
desc-archive: Documentation Center and Museum on Migration in Germany (DOMiD).
desc-stats: NRW state database: Statistics and demography Cologne-Mülheim.
org-1-desc: - Solidarity alliance of those affected and civil society.
org-2-desc: - Research group for the documentation of the district history in Mülheim.
body-about: As a psychologist at the interface with programming, I develop analytical approaches for dealing with complex issues. This project is a research project from my studies (Code & Context) in collaboration with la Initiative Keupstraße ist überall, which deals with current social problems. This focus formed the basis for the connection between technical digital implementation and psychological research.
footer-disclaimer: NOTE: This project is an ongoing process. The analyses are constantly being supplemented as new data is developed. The current status does not represent a final evaluation.
scrolly-0: In this state of shock, you don't realize anything.
scrolly-1: But for us, that was just the beginning.
scrolly-3: It is difficult to put this painful experience into words.
scrolly-4: When I got to the front, I realized the extent of the explosion.
scrolly-5: Of course I still feel shocked by yesterday's crime. When I close my eyes, I can still see the images from yesterday.
scrolly-6: We endured all of this until 2011.
scrolly-7: You're a victim, but you're under immense pressure. My psyche was completely ruined.
scrolly-8: On June 9, 2004, I was at home with my 7-year-old son. I heard a loud bang and thought it was an earthquake.
scrolly-9: The findings that our security authorities have made so far do not point to a terrorist background, but to a criminal milieu
scrolly-10: After two hours, the police suddenly stormed my apartment without ringing the doorbell. They broke down two doors and searched my apartment.
scrolly-11: but the investigations have not yet been completed, so I cannot make a final assessment of these events at this time.
scrolly-12: The police told me that I and my husband were suspected of having planted a bomb. The reason they gave was that I was Kurdish and therefore suspected of having something to do with the PKK.
scrolly-13: To be honest: Whenever I talk about it, the memories of that day come back to me. Even now that the German state is talking to us about it, we are still under the influence of the attack
scrolly-14: The attack in the Keupstraße in June 2004 and the state failure in the NSU context have painfully revived and intensified these memories for many of those affected
scrolly-15: Physically, I still have a scar here on my hand. I also have a permanent scar on the back of my head. And I have scars in two places on my leg.
scrolly-16: This becomes clear, among other things, in the results of a survey by the Dortmund Futureorg Institute of over 1,000 migrants of Turkish origin living in Germany on the effects of the NSU murders
scrolly-17: We were invited to Berlin three or four times... They apologized to us, the heads of state. But what happened, happened. Apologies are useless. We have suffered material and psychological wounds.
scrolly-18: I had problems with my ear for a year or two. I didn't go to the doctor about it. A few of my colleagues were at the police station and they told me how they were treated there, how the police tried to find culprits and suspects
scrolly-19: Great injustice has been done to these people
scrolly-20: If I had gone to the doctor, the doctor would have asked me where I work and where I live. And if I had said that I lived on Keupstraße, he would have said: "Aha, you have something to do with the bomb that exploded."
scrolly-21: Now they are getting the appreciation from mainstream society that they need. This could restore the security and trust they have lost. Traumatized people would pass on their experiences to the next generation. The "creation of a culture of remembrance" is an important step.
scrolly-22: That's why I waited. Later, I went to the doctor and he told me that nothing could be done now because it was too late. I can no longer hear properly.
scrolly-23: I was shocked. The first two years were chaotic for me. I was constantly afraid[...]I expect the story to be properly illuminated in the process. They may not go in really deep. If it doesn't come to a court verdict, if nothing is proven in court or only one woman has to go to prison and there are no further consequences, it will be bad
scrolly-24: I only heard the screams of my friends Attila, Tamar and Hasan.
scrolly-25: There were two bombs. One had this force with the nails that were in it, and the other was simply the justice system, the system, the rule of law, which didn't work.
scrolly-26: The wounds are secret, I no longer feel any physical pain, but the emotional pain we suffered remains. They have remained!
scrolly-27: Not much has changed. Some politicians came, posed for photos and left again. We are lonely again, but at least we are no longer suspects
scrolly-28: I'm really fed up with all the reports. People just don't feel like saying the same thing over and over again, because it's about feelings and not about something you've memorized
char-hasan-desc: Born in Ankara, came to Germany in 1997. Already worked as a hairdresser in Turkey and also works in this profession in Germany. Worked in Keupstraße for about eight years.
char-ozcan-role: Owner of the Özcan hair studio
char-ozcan-desc: He came to Germany in the early 1990s from Turkey, where he trained as a kuaför (Turkish for hairdresser). He is married and has two children; his wife Aygül is from Cologne.
char-testigo1-desc: was a customer in the salon on June 9.
char-testigo2-role: Former chairwoman of the Keupstraße interest group
char-testigo2-desc: Born in Cologne in 1971, daughter of Turkish immigrants, businesswoman and chairwoman of IG Keupstraße.
char-schily-desc: He was Federal Minister of the Interior from 1998 to 2005. He was a co-founder of the Green Party, from which he switched to the SPD in November 1989.
char-man-desc: Has lived in Germany for 20 years. As he does not want any conclusions to be drawn about his person, he does not state his profession.
char-kleffner-desc: From 2004 to 2009, she headed the mobile counseling service for victims of right-wing violence in Saxony-Anhalt. Until 2013, she was an advisor to the parliamentary group Die Linke in the Bundestag on the NSU Committee of Inquiry.
char-gün-desc: Psychologist (University of Cologne) with a focus on therapy and counseling. He represents migrants in health issues and leads working groups on migration and psychiatry at the LVR.
char-muhamed-desc: Has lived in Germany for 30 years
